Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (acting by shareholder Carolyn Fong in S 477/2012)

Facts.Text: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore.
Suit No 477 of 2012 is a derivative action brought on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

Suit No 477 of 2012 was commenced by shareholder Carolyn Fong after she obtained leave under section 216A of the Companies Act to institute a derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In Originating Summons No 505 of 2010, the High Court stated that Peter Fong was the controlling mind and will of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd at the material times for the purpose of granting leave under section 216A of the Companies Act.
The decision of the High Court to grant Carolyn Fong leave to commence the derivative action was affirmed by the Court of Appeal.
In Suit No 477 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd claims that certain business opportunities were wrongfully diverted by Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In Suit No 477 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd alleges that Linda Kao Chai-Chau breached fiduciary duties in respect of certain transactions which mostly took place while Peter Fong was alive and Chairman of the company.
In the derivative action, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong does not deny that Peter Fong was the alter ego of the company.
In the derivative action, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong accepts that Peter Fong had at least ostensible authority to give instructions on behalf of the company.
In the derivative action, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong contends that Peter Fong did not know of the transactions in question and or that the transactions were unauthorised.

Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong objected to Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s application in Summons No 2990 of 2013 for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong in the derivative action.

Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong submitted that the third party proceedings in the derivative action were unnecessary because the claim against the Estate of Peter Fong replicated the core of Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s defence.

Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong argued before the Assistant Registrar that an indemnity could not be claimed in respect of an illegal act constituting an offence under section 157 of the Companies Act.
By 2006, approximately 51% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd had been transferred to the Fong Foundation charitable trust.
At the date of Peter Fong’s death, he held about 3.6% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
At the date of Peter Fong’s death, the Fong Foundation held about 51% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
At the date of Peter Fong’s death, the remaining shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd were held in varying amounts by 11 other persons including Carolyn Fong who owned about 6.2% of the shares.

In the derivative action, one claim for which contribution is sought by Linda Kao Chai-Chau concerns alleged diversions of business opportunities from Chinese chemical companies between 2000 and the date of the writ.
In the derivative action, a second claim for which contribution is sought concerns alleged diversions of business opportunities from another Chinese company from around 2001.
In the derivative action, a third claim for which contribution is sought concerns alleged diversions occurring around 2002 and 2004.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Linda Kao Chai-Chau may pursue third party claims for indemnity or contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of alleged diversions of business opportunities and alleged breaches of fiduciary duty owed to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Under Order 16 rule 1 of the Rules of Court, a defendant who claims contribution or indemnity or related relief against a non-party may issue a third party notice, and under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act, any person liable in respect of damage suffered by another may seek contribution from any other person liable in respect of the same damage, with the court retaining discretion over the extent of contribution including a complete indemnity.
Party_Details.Application: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong opposed the issuance of a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong on the basis that the third party claim duplicated Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s defence that she acted on Peter Fong’s instructions, that Peter Fong’s knowledge and authority were insufficient to support an indemnity claim in light of alleged illegality under section 157 of the Companies Act, and that if Peter Fong had authorised the transactions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau would not be liable to the company in the first place.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The High Court allowed the commencement and amendment of third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong notwithstanding the objections raised by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered the statutory framework for third party proceedings under Order 16 of the Rules of Court and the contribution regime under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act, and concluded that statutory contribution is not barred by illegality in the manner suggested by the plaintiff, that the ex turpi causa principle is accommodated through the court’s discretion on the quantum of contribution rather than as a threshold bar, and that the third party claim was not redundant because there existed a realistic scenario in which Linda Kao Chai-Chau could be held in breach of her independent fiduciary duties to the company while the Estate of Peter Fong could also be liable in respect of the same damage, thereby justifying the ventilation of contribution issues at trial.

Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (acting by Receivers and Managers in S 1015/2012)

Facts.Text: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is a company whose business generated substantial revenue prior to the disputes.
Ernst & Young was appointed as Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd on 17 January 2012.
The appointment of the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd was made pursuant to an application by shareholder Carolyn Fong.
A consent order was granted following an agreement between Carolyn Fong and Linda Kao Chai-Chau in relation to the appointment of the Receivers and Managers.

After their appointment, the Receivers and Managers formed the view that there was evidence of other diversions made by Linda Kao Chai-Chau and others to the detriment of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
The Receivers and Managers commenced Suit No 1015 of 2012 against Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 other individuals and corporate entities.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers allege that Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 others were in a conspiracy to divert business away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers also bring a claim for breach of fiduciary duty.
The transactions and events complained of by the Receivers and Managers in Suit No 1015 of 2012 largely concern events occurring after the death of Peter Fong on 25 April 2008.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers expressly denies that Peter Fong was the alter ego or controlling mind of the company.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau commenced third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong on 19 April 2013 seeking an indemnity.
On 12 June 2013, the Receivers and Managers applied by Summons No 2989 of 2013 to set aside the third party proceedings in Suit No 1015 of 2012.
On 19 July 2013, the Assistant Registrar granted Summons No 2989 of 2013 and set aside the third party notice in Suit No 1015 of 2012.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers submitted that the third party proceedings were unnecessary because they overlapped with Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s pleaded defence.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers argued that a breach of director’s duty is a criminal offence under section 157 of the Companies Act.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers referred to possible offences of dishonest misappropriation of property and criminal breach of trust under the Penal Code in relation to the alleged conduct.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers submitted that the rule in Burrows v Rhodes and Jameson should apply to bar an indemnity claim in respect of acts constituting such offences.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers submitted that even if Peter Fong had directed the alleged conspiracy, Linda Kao Chai-Chau as managing director owed an independent duty to the company and could not avoid that duty by following his instructions.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers argued that the third party claim was inherently improbable because it would require Peter Fong, as alleged alter ego and controlling mind, to have instructed acts that would cause the company to lose almost its entire business.

After the appeals on the third party proceedings were heard, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by Carolyn Fong applied for leave to hand over conduct of the derivative action to the Receivers and Managers, and the Receivers and Managers took a neutral position on that application.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd must accommodate third party proceedings by Linda Kao Chai-Chau against the Estate of Peter Fong for indemnity or contribution in Suit No 1015 of 2012, despite their objections based on illegality, redundancy, and procedural prejudice.
Party_Details.Rule: A plaintiff may object to third party proceedings where they would cause undue embarrassment, expense, or delay, but where a defendant seeks contribution under section 15 of the Civil Law Act from another person liable in respect of the same damage, the statutory right to contribution is generally available regardless of whether the underlying conduct may constitute a tort or crime, subject to the court’s discretion under section 16 to adjust or eliminate contribution.
Party_Details.Application: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers objected to the third party notice on the basis that the alleged breaches of director’s duty and related conduct were potentially criminal, that any indemnity would be barred by public policy, and that any fault of Peter Fong would already be relevant to the assessment of Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s liability and damages, but the court determined that the statutory contribution regime allowed a contribution claim even where the underlying acts might be criminal and that the possibility of overlapping factual issues did not render the third party proceedings redundant.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The High Court allowed the continuation and amendment of the third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong in Suit No 1015 of 2012 notwithstanding the objections of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting by the Receivers and Managers.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that the Receivers and Managers were entitled to raise objections based on potential delay, complexity, and overlap with the main action, but concluded that the statutory framework for contribution under the Civil Law Act was intended to permit claims even where the underlying conduct might be tortious or criminal, that the ex turpi causa principle should be addressed through the trial court’s discretion on contribution rather than as a preliminary bar, and that there was a realistic scenario in which both Linda Kao Chai-Chau and the Estate of Peter Fong could be liable in respect of the same damage, such that the third party issues should be determined at trial rather than struck out at the interlocutory stage.

Party_Details.Name: Kao Chai-Chau Linda

Facts.Text: Linda Kao Chai-Chau was appointed managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in or about 1996 by its majority shareholder and then Chairman, Peter Fong.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau served as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd from 1996 up to 2012.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau is the defendant in Suit No 477 of 2012, the derivative action brought on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau is the defendant in Suit No 1015 of 2012 brought by the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In both Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s pleaded defence is that the transactions complained of were carried out in accordance with the instructions of Peter Fong.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau asserts that all the business opportunities she is alleged to have diverted were not business opportunities of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau claims that, acting on the instructions of Peter Fong, she received and disbursed funds on behalf of Peter Fong’s friends and associates as a favour to them.
For transactions not made on specific instructions of Peter Fong, Linda Kao Chai-Chau claims that they were made pursuant to standing instructions or an understanding with Peter Fong.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau contends that Peter Fong was at the material times the majority shareholder, Chairman, and controlling mind and will of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In the derivative action, Linda Kao Chai-Chau applied by Summons No 2990 of 2013 for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity.
In the Receivers and Managers action, Linda Kao Chai-Chau commenced third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong on 19 April 2013 seeking an indemnity.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau sought to amend the third party notice in the Receivers and Managers action by Summons No 4420 of 2013 to add a claim for contribution in addition to indemnity.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau sought to amend the third party notice in the derivative action by Summons No 4419 of 2013 to add a claim for contribution in addition to indemnity.

On 19 July 2013, the Assistant Registrar refused to grant Linda Kao Chai-Chau leave to commence third party proceedings in the derivative action and set aside the third party notice in the Receivers and Managers action.
Registrar’s Appeal No 247 of 2013 is Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s appeal in the Receivers and Managers action against the setting aside of the third party notice.
Registrar’s Appeal No 246 of 2013 is Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s appeal in the derivative action against the refusal of leave to commence third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong.

Linda Kao Chai-Chau bases her claim for contribution on section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act, which allows a person liable in respect of damage suffered by another to recover contribution from any other person liable in respect of the same damage.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau submits that the right to contribution she relies on is statutory rather than derived from the common law.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau submits that the statutory contribution regime under the Civil Law Act is not barred by illegality in the manner suggested by the plaintiffs.

Linda Kao Chai-Chau submits that even if Peter Fong is regarded as the alter ego of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, this does not necessarily absolve her from liability for breach of director’s and fiduciary duties.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Linda Kao Chai-Chau, as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, is entitled to pursue third party claims for indemnity or contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong under Order 16 of the Rules of Court and sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act in respect of alleged diversions of business opportunities and alleged breaches of fiduciary and director’s duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Order 16 rule 1 of the Rules of Court permits a defendant to issue a third party notice against a non-party from whom the defendant claims contribution, indemnity, or related relief connected with the original subject matter of the action, and section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act provides that any person liable in respect of damage suffered by another may recover contribution from any other person liable in respect of the same damage, whether jointly or otherwise, with section 16(2) conferring discretion on the court to determine the amount of contribution including a complete indemnity.
Party_Details.Application: Linda Kao Chai-Chau, facing claims that she diverted business opportunities and breached fiduciary and director’s duties, invoked Order 16 rule 1 to bring the Estate of Peter Fong into both suits on the basis that Peter Fong, as Chairman and alleged controlling mind of the company, instructed or authorised the impugned transactions and thereby may be liable in respect of the same damage to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, and she relied on section 15 of the Civil Law Act to argue that she is entitled to seek contribution or an effective indemnity from the Estate of Peter Fong if she is held liable in the main actions.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The High Court allowed Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s appeals to commence and maintain third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong and granted her applications to amend the third party notices to include claims for contribution in addition to indemnity.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court rejected the Assistant Registrar’s view that Linda Kao Chai-Chau was legally precluded from seeking indemnity or contribution because knowledge of illegality would be imputed to her, and instead held that the statutory contribution regime under the Civil Law Act supersedes the common law bar described in Burrows v Rhodes and Jameson, that ex turpi causa concerns can be addressed through the trial court’s discretion under section 16 to adjust or deny contribution, and that the third party claims were not redundant because there exists a plausible scenario in which Linda Kao Chai-Chau could be held in breach of her independent fiduciary duties while Peter Fong, as Chairman and director, could also be found to have breached his duties by directing or authorising the same transactions, thereby making both potentially liable in respect of the same damage and justifying the determination of contribution issues at trial.
